shock.title: Understanding Initial Grief
shock.content: The first days and weeks after loss can feel surreal. You might feel numb, disbelieving, or in shock. This is your mind's way of protecting you from overwhelming pain. There's no timeline for this stage - be patient with yourself.

shock.title: Self-Care in Early Days
shock.content:
During this time:
• Accept help from others
• Don't make major decisions if possible
• Eat and sleep as best you can
• Allow yourself to feel whatever comes
• Lean on your support system
• Be gentle with yourself

anger.title: Processing Difficult Emotions
anger.content: Anger is a natural part of grief. You might feel angry at the situation, at medical providers, at yourself, or even at your loved one for leaving. These feelings are normal and valid.

anger.title: Healthy Expression
anger.content:
Ways to process anger:
• Physical activity (if able)
• Writing or journaling
• Talking with a counselor
• Creative expression
• Support groups where others understand

Avoid directing anger harmfully at yourself or others.

bargaining.title: Making Sense of Loss
bargaining.content: You might find yourself thinking 'what if' or 'if only.' This is bargaining - trying to make sense of the loss or wishing you could change the outcome. This is a normal part of processing grief.

bargaining.title: Finding Meaning
bargaining.content:
While we can't change what happened, we can:
• Honor their memory
• Find ways to carry their legacy forward
• Learn from the experience
• Eventually find peace with what is

depression.title: Coping with Sadness
depression.content: Deep sadness is a natural response to loss. You might feel empty, exhausted, or wonder if you'll ever feel joy again. These feelings are part of the healing process, though they can be overwhelming.

depression.title: When to Seek Help
depression.content:
Reach out for professional help if:
• You can't function in daily life
• You have thoughts of self-harm
• Depression lasts for many months without improvement
• You're isolating completely
• You're unable to care for yourself

Depression is treatable - you don't have to suffer alone.

acceptance.title: Moving Forward
acceptance.content: Acceptance doesn't mean forgetting or being 'over it.' It means learning to live with the loss, finding a new normal, and allowing joy back into your life while still honoring your loved one's memory.

acceptance.title: Honoring Memory
acceptance.content:
Ways to honor their memory:
• Share stories about them
• Continue traditions they loved
• Support causes they cared about
• Live in a way that would make them proud
• Allow yourself to smile when remembering them

hotlines.title: Crisis Support
hotlines.content:
National Suicide Prevention Lifeline: 988
Crisis Text Line: Text HOME to 741741
Grief Support Hotline: 1-800-445-4808

hotlines.title: 24/7 Support
hotlines.content:
These services are available anytime:
• SAMHSA National Helpline: 1-800-662-4357
• Disaster Distress Helpline: 1-800-985-5990
• Veterans Crisis Line: 988 then press 1

support_groups.title: Finding Support Groups
support_groups.content:
Support groups connect you with others who understand:
• Local hospice organizations often offer free grief groups
• Online communities provide 24/7 connection
• Faith-based organizations may offer grief support
• Specialized groups exist for specific types of loss

Ask your hospice social worker for local recommendations.

support_groups.title: Online Communities
support_groups.content:
Online grief support can be accessed anytime:
• GriefShare.org
• The Compassionate Friends
• What's Your Grief community
• Reddit r/GriefSupport

Remember to protect your privacy and emotional well-being online.

professional_help.title: Finding a Grief Counselor
professional_help.content:
Professional support can be invaluable:
• Ask your hospice for referrals
• Check with your insurance for covered providers
• Look for counselors specializing in grief/bereavement
• Consider both individual and group therapy
• Telehealth options are widely available

You deserve support during this difficult time.

professional_help.title: Types of Therapy
professional_help.content:
Different approaches that may help:
• Grief counseling (short-term, focused)
• Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT)
• Complicated grief therapy
• Art or music therapy
• EMDR for traumatic loss

A therapist can help you find the right approach for you.
